Facts.text: Eastern Roman Emperor Justinian appoints a commission to codify  the works of classical jurists (the Digest published 533)
Byzantine General Belisarius defeats the Vandals, under King Gelimer, at the Battle of Ticameron
St Sergius I begins his reign as Catholic Pope succeeding Conon
Chancellor Haimeric selects pope (Lamberto becomes Honorius II)
Sicilian chancellor Stephen du Perche moves the royal court to Messina to prevent a rebellion.
Hulagu Khan captures and destroys Hashshashin stronghold at Alamut, in present-day Iran, part of the Mongol offensive on Islamic southwest Asia
Bishop Jonah of Moscow chosen as the new Metropolitan of Kiev, for the first time by the bishops of Moscow not the Patriarch of Constantinople
Stephen III of Moldavia defeats Matthias Corvinus of Hungary, with the latter being injured thrice, at the Battle of Baia
Leidse university names Rembert Dodoens professor of botany and medicine
Laevinus Torrentius, becomes bishop of Antwerp
State of Holland grants a patent for a windmill with a crankshaft
German astronomer Simon Marius is the first to observe the Andromeda Galaxy through a telescope
Duke of Braganca crowned King Johan IV of Portugal
Philippines: Andres Malongs rebels plunders Bagnotan
Brandenburg declares himself neutral in Devolutie War
King Philip's War: After peace talk fail, Narraganset warriors attack the Jireh Bull Blockhouse, in South Kingston, Rhode Island, killing at least 15 colonists
Tax revolt on Terschelling due to tax on cereal
Battle at Kesseldorf: Prussian force beats Saksen & Austria near Dresden
1st US law school established at University of Pennsylvania
1st life insurance policy issued in US (Philadelphia)
Revolutionary Tribunal abolished in France
1st Irish magazine in US, "Shamrock" published
1st General pharmacopoeia in US published, Boston
Patent Office burns in Washington, D.C.
1st street-cleaning machine in the US used in Philadelphia
GR Kirchoff describes chemical composition of the Sun
Romania's mountain railway, from Anina to Oravita, opens
Skirmish at Bean's Station, Tennessee (Knoxville Campaign)
Battle of Nashville, Tennessee: Union forces under General George Thomas shatters General John Bell Hood's Confederate Army of Tennessee
Raid on Stoneman: Abingdon & Glade Springs, Virginia
Shogunate rebels found Ezo Republic in Hokkaidō
1st reigning king to visit US is the King of Hawaii, received by President Grant

Henry Bergh, Eldgridge Gerry, and philanthropist John D. Wright found the Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children in NYC, the world's first anti-child abuse agency. Later known as The New York Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children (NYSPCC).
Day 2 of first cricket test between Australia and England, Australia 586 runs (Gregory 201) England 3-130
Battle at Colenso, South Africa: British army defeated by Boer force
German agricultural tariffs are increased
NY Wall Street vendor Italo Marchiony [Marcioni] granted patent for a mound for making ice cream cones
The Pushkin House is established in St. Petersburg to preserve the cultural heritage of Alexander Pushkin
In Persia, the Shah leads a coup de'etat against the liberal Prime Minister Nasir ul-Mulk and imprisons him, but a popular uprising forces the Shah to restore Nasir ul-Mulk soon after
Thomas J. Lynch becomes president of baseball's National League
Nicaragua becomes a signatory to the Buenos Aires copyright treaty
Battle of Lodz ends; Russians retreat toward Moscow
British fleet forfeits chance to destroy German fleet in North Sea
Serbian troops retake Belgrade in Austria-Hungary
Battle of Verdun: French launch major offensive against German lines after a six-day artillery bombardment
Moldavian Republic declares independence from Russia
World War I: An armistice is reached between the new Russian Bolshevik government and the Central Powers
American Jewish Congress holds its 1st meeting
Edna St Vincent Millay's play "Aria da Capo" premieres in NYC
Fiume (Rijeka, modern Croatia) declares its Independence
IVVV (association) peace congress on war forms in Hague
1st hockey game at Madison Square Garden, NYC: Montreal Canadiens 3, NY Americans 1
1st road with a depressed trough (Texas) opens to traffic
Fascist national symbol elevated in Italy
William Edward Hickman kidnaps 12 year-old Marion Parker in Los Angeles, whom he later kills
Baseball owners agree to ban Sunday doubleheaders until after June 15
In major upset, Dutch grand master Max Euwe becomes World Chess Champion beating Russian Alexander Alekhine (9 wins, 13 draws, 8 losses) in Zandvoort, Netherlands
KVL-AM in Seattle Washington changes call letters to KEEN (now KING)
Groundbreaking begins for Jefferson Memorial in Washington, D.C.
1st commercial manufacture of nylon yarn in Seaford, Delaware
Gas/electrical use restricted in Holland
German submarine U-127 sinks
North Africa: allied assault up Italians Gazala-posing
The American Federation of Labor adopts a no-strike policy in war industries.
USS Swordfish becomes 1st US sub to sink a Japanese ship
Massachusetts issues 1st US vehicular license plate tabs
Hizbu'allah (Arm forces for Allah) forms
US troops land on Mindoro (Philippines)
John J "Cardinal" O'Connor, ordained as a priest
Giants Filchock & Hapes suspended by NFL, didn't report bribe attempt
Former state department official Alger Hiss indicted in NYC for perjury
NYC's Port Authority opens
Christine Jorgenson is the first known American to undergo a sex-change operation
KHON TV channel 2 in Honolulu, HI (NBC) begins broadcasting
WJHG TV channel 7 in Panama City, FL (NBC/ABC) begins broadcasting
Fordham University scraps football team for financial reasons
Netherlands Antilles becomes co-equal part of Kingdom of Netherlands
US Scientists involved in secret Project Superpressure at GE Research Labs realized they have synthesized diamonds for the first time
Emergency crisis in North Ireland proclaimed after IRA strikes
KGW TV channel 8 in Portland, OR (NBC) begins broadcasting
WRAL TV channel 5 in Raleigh-Durham, NC (CBS) begins broadcasting
Equal access rule, US political parties get TV broadcasting time
L. J. Suenens appointed archbishop of Mechelen-Brussels, Belgium
Canada's House of Commons votes 163 to 78 to approve the red Maple Leaf Flag
3rd cyclone of year kills 15,000 at the mouths of the Ganges River in Bangladesh
Gemini 6 launched; makes 1st rendezvous in space (with Gemini 7)
Audouin Dollfus discovers 10th satellite of Saturn, Janus
John W. Mecom Jr becomes 1st owner of New Orlean Saints
The Beatles release fan-club single "Christmas Time is Here Again"
The Silver Bridge collapses over the Ohio River in the US, killing 46 people
San Francisco Fire Department replaces leather helmets with plastic ones
Illinois State Constitution is adopted at a special election
Robert Wilson and Alan Lloyd's musical theatre work "Deafman Glance" first performance at Center for New Performing Arts in Iowa City, Iowa
South Korean ferry Namyong-Ho sinks in Strait of Korea, 308 killed
Soviet Venera 7 is the first spacecraft to land on another planet (Venus)
Striking shipyard workers in Gdańsk, Poland set ablaze the ruling Provincial Committee's building; responding soldiers kill 8
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
American kidnap victim John Paul Getty III freed after ransom paid by oil tycoon grandfather John Paul Getty
American Psychiatric Association declares homosexuality is not a mental illness
Pirates of Caribbean ride opens at Disneyland
Sandy Hawley becomes 1st jockey to win 500 races in 1 year
Tennessee beats Temple 11-6 in low scoring NCAA basketball game
Jamaica premier Manley wins elections
Oil tanker Argo Merchant runs aground on the Nantucket Shoals, spilling 7.6 m million gallons of fuel oil - one of the largest oil spills at sea
Samoa becomes a member of the UN
Saint Maarten Patriotic Movement (SPM) forms under W. James
Test Cricket debut of Malcolm Marshall, v India at Bangalore
World Court in Hague rules Iran should release all US hostages
Premier Queddei troops conquers Chad capital N'djamena
ZBZ Sangha registered after 5 yrs of administrative hassles in Warsaw
NASA launches Intelsat V satellite, no. 503
Sao Tome & Principe constitution approved
Spain reopens border with Gibraltar
Teamsters president Roy Williams and 4 others convicted of bribery
Last 80 US combat soldiers in Grenada withdrawn
MLB suspends 3 Kansas City Royals players - Willie Wilson, Willie Aikens, and Jerry Martin - due to cocaine usage
Space shuttle Columbia flies to Kennedy Space Center via El Paso, Kelly AFB
USSR launches Vega 1 for rendezvous with Halley's Comet
150 killed during race riot in Karachi
Carnegie Hall reopens after a $50 million facelift
First National US tour of musical "Les Misérables" opens at Shubert Theatre, Boston, Massachusetts
WNew AM (1130) NYC resigns air, replaced by WBBR
C-130 flies into a Philippines hill & explodes, 16 killed
Haitian premier Robert Malval resigns
John Williams makes his final appearance as conductor of Boston Pops
Lee Aspen resigns as secretary of defense
Y-12 crashes at Phonesavanh, Laos: 18 killed
John Bruton becomes Ireland's premier
Liberia militia kills 48 inhabitants of Monrovia
South Pacific island nation of Palau becomes a member of the UN
Playboy goes back on sale after 36 year ban in Ireland

The European Communities Court of Justice hands down the "Bosman ruling", giving EU footballers the right to a free transfer at the end of their contracts, with the provision that they are transferring from one UEFA Federation to another.
The Leaning Tower of Pisa reopens after 11 years and $27,000,000 to fortify it, without fixing its famous lean
68th Heisman Trophy Award: Carson Palmer, USC (QB)
Argentina's president Néstor Kirchner announces the early repayment of its external debt to the IMF.
Introduction of the F-22 Raptor into USAF active service.
Latvia amends its constitution to eliminate possibility of same-sex couples being entitled to marry.
The 2005 Atlantic Power Outage began.
First flight of the F-35 Lightning II.
74th Heisman Trophy Award: Sam Bradford, Oklahoma (QB)
Boeing's new 787 Dreamliner makes its maiden flight from Seattle, Washington.
ESPN and the NCAA extend their TV rights deal through 2023-24, giving the network worldwide multi-media rights to 24 NCAA championships for various sports; deal worth $500m
18 Asylum seekers drown in the Aegean Sea on the way to Greece from Turkey
China successfully lands its moon rover on the moon
1,000 schools closed in Los Angeles after a email threat, supposedly from jihadists
Fifth Republican presidential candidates debate in Las Vegas, Nevada
Mayor of Flint, Michigan declares state of emergency over contaminated water supplies amid calls for a criminal investigation
Ceasefire declared in Aleppo to allow hundreds to be evacutated from last rebel held area
White supremacist Dylann Roof is found guilty of the Charleston Church Massacre which killed 9
Pakistani teenager Ahed Tamimi filmed slapping Israeli officers during protest, arrested 4 days later
Affordable Care Act ('Obamacare') ruled unconstitutional by a federal judge in Fort Worth, Texas; later reversed by US Supreme Court
Diplomats from 200 countries agree to a set of rules to implement the UN Paris Climate Agreement in Katowice, Poland
Egyptian Archaeologists announce discovery of 4,400 year old tomb of Fifth Dynasty priest in Saqqara pyramid complex near Cairo
Longest ever UN climate talks end in Madrid with compromise deal on curbing carbon only, other targets deferred for a year
Protests against India's new citizenship bill that excludes Muslims, erupt across major cities, killing at least five
Raiders play their final NFL game in Oakland conceding 17 unanswered 2nd half points to go down 20-16 to the Jacksonville Jaguars; team to play out of Las Vegas in 2020
COVID-19 Omicron variant called "probably the most significant threat" of the pandemic by head of UK Health Security Agency, warning of "staggering" growth in next few days
Tens of thousands of nurses go on strike in England, Wales and Northern Ireland in largest strike in NHS history
American jazz vocal quartet "The Manhattan Transfer" marks their 50th anniversary with a farewell concert at Disney Hall in Los Angeles, California
New report reveals homelessness in the US increased by 12%, with more than 650,000 people experiencing homeless one night in January 2023
State memorial held for Australian entertainer Barry Humphries (behind Dame Edna Everage character) at Sydney Opera House
Five members of the "Bali Nine", arresting for smuggling drugs out of Indonesia in 2005, return to Australia after serving nearly 20 years in prison
More than 50 people are killed in Gaza in Israeli airstrikes as the death toll for Palestinians killed in the Israel-Gaza War surpasses 45,000
Bartolomeu Diasreturns to Portugal after becoming 1st known European to sail round the Cape of Good Hope
Hunted byQueen Elizabeth Ifor treason in "The Rising of the North", Charles Neville, Earl of Westmorland, escapes to Scotland.
Italian composerGioachino Rossini(24) gets commission from Teatro Argentina to write his opera "Almaviva", later known as "Il barbiere di Siviglia" (The Barber of Seville)
Napoleon Bonapartereceives a French state funeral in Paris 19 years after his death
Preview performance ofAntonín Dvořák's Symphony No. 9 - "New World Symphony" draws a crowd to Carnegie Hall, New York City
Swiss pilotWalter Mittelholzeris the first to fly over Mt. Kilimanjaro
Dutch airplane the 'Snip' (Fokker F.XVIII) departs the Netherlands for the 1st flight to Paramaribo and Curacao, takes 55 hours 58 minutes
"Gone With The Wind", drama film directed byVictor Flemingand starringClark GableandVivien Leigh, premieres in Atlanta (Best Picture 1940, inflation not adjusted highest-grossing film of all time)
American bandleader MajorGlenn Miller, lost over the English Channel
US Congress gives GeneralEisenhowerhis 5th star
Albert Camus' play "The Just Assassins (Les Justes)" premieres in Paris
PopePius XIIpublishes encyclical Orientales Ecclesias
"Folsom Prison Blues" single released byJohnny Cash(Billboard Song of the Year 1968)
Ballon d'Or: Real Madrid forwardAlfredo Di Stéfanowins his second award for best football player in Europe ahead of teammateRaymond Kopaand Juventus' Welsh utility John Charles
Everly Brothers record "Let It Be Me", an English translation of Gilbert Bécaud's French song "Je t'appartiens"; reaches No. 7 on the charts
Martin Luther King Jr.joins over 500 civil rights demonstrators in jail, arrested for protesting in Albany, Georgia
US PresidentJohn F. KennedyandJacqueline Kennedyvisit Puerto Rico - last official sitting presidential visit until Barack Obama in 2011
Comedian-impressionistVaughn Meader's "1st Family" album goes #1 & stays #1 for 12 weeks
DutchQueen Julianaopens Zeeland Bridge to Oosterschelde
William Eckert replacesFord Frickas 4th Commissioner of Baseball

John LennonandYoko Ono's Plastic Ono Band, play their only European gig - appearing in UNICEF charity concert at London's Lyceum Ballroom, band is augmented by Bonnie & Delaney Bramlett, guitaristsGeorge HarrisonandEric Clapton, and keyboardistBilly Preston
Stan SmithandRod Laverfinish with 4-1 records atop inaugural season ending ATP Masters Grand Prix tennis standings in Tokyo, Japan; Smith wins event after beating Laver 4–6, 6–3, 6–4 in the rounds
Guillermo Vilasof Argentina beats defending champion lie Năstase 7–6, 6–2, 3–6, 3–6, 6–4 to win his first and only season-ending ATP Masters Grand Prix tennis final in Melbourne, Australia
German label Ariola Records releases eponymous debut album "Rosanne Cash", produced by future husband Rodney Crowell
British rockerDavid Bowieperforms three songs on American television program Saturday Night Live - "The Man Who Sold the World", "TVC-15" and "Boys Keep Swinging"
Chris Haneyand Scott Abbott develop the board game Trivial Pursuit
Deposed Shah of IranMohammad Reza Pahlavileaves US for Panama
NY YankeeDave Winfieldbecomes highest-paid player, 10 years $15M
Bill Parcellsbecomes 12th head coach of NY Giants
Wendy Wasserstein's play "Isn't It Romantic" premieres in NYC
CIA directorWilliam J. Caseysuffers a cerebral seizure
Lori Davis of Long Island suesMike Tysonfor grabbing her buttocks
Arthur Asheis named Sports Illustrated Sportsperson of the Year
British Prime MinisterJohn Majorand Irish  Taoiseach Albert Reynolds sign Downing Street Declaration concerning Northern Ireland self determination
"Heat" directed by Michael Mann and starringRobert De Niro,Al PacinoandVal Kilmer, is released in the US
"Spice World" movie directed by Bob Spiers and starring girl pop group theSpice Girlspremieres in the UK
SF 49ers retireJoe Montana's #16
"Million Dollar Baby", directed byClint Eastwoodand starringHilary SwankandMorgan Freemanis released (Best Picture 2005)
American blues legendB.B. Kingis awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom by US PresidentGeorge W. Bush
Barry Bondsis sentenced to 30 days of house arrest, two years of probation and 250 hours of community service, for an obstruction of justice conviction stemming from a grand jury appearance in 2003
Former French PresidentJacques Chiracis convicted of diverting public funds, receives a two-year suspended prison sentence
Michelle Bacheletis re-elected President of Chile
Anita Hill is appointed to lead a Hollywood commission on sexual harassment, spearheaded byKathleen Kennedy
Ukrainian PresidentPetro Poroshenkoannounces establishment of independent Ukrainian Orthodox Church, splitting from Russian Orthodox
Anna Wintour, American Vogue editor-in-chief, is named as Condé Nast's first-ever global Chief Content Officer
MacKenzie Scottannounces she has given away more than $4 billion to 384 nonprofit organizations across the US
UK newspaper "The Sun" publishes recording ofTom Cruiseberating his film crew for breaking COVID-19 protocols
Bruce Springsteensells his music back catalogue to Sony Music Entertainment for an estimated $500 million, in possibly the biggest deal ever for a single artist
Las Vegas' McCarran Airport officially changes name toHarry ReidInternational Airport
Mexico's Maya Train inaugurated by PresidentAndrés Manuel López Obrador, first part of a $20 billion, 950-mile line tourist train route around the Yucatan peninsular
US jury orders American attorneyRudy Giulianito pay $148M  to two former Georgia election workers in civil defamation suit for spreading lies about them following 2020 presidential election
Spanish Netherlands, Denmark and Norway adopt the Gregorian calendar
US Bill of Rights ratified when Virginia gives its approval, becomes amendments 1-10 of the US constitution
ANZAC forces begin their withdrawal from the Gallipoli Peninsula after Ottoman forces successfully defend access to Constantinople
Court in Israel sentences Nazi party officialAdolf Eichmannto death for war crimes for his participation in organizing the Holocaust
Prince Munetaka, 6th Japanese shōgun of the Kamakura shogunate (1252-66), born in Heian Kyō, Japan (d. 1274)
Lucas Osiander, German composer, born in the Free Imperial City of Nuremberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1604)
Christoph Demantius, German composer, born in Reichenberg, Duchy of Bavaria (d. 1643)
Samuel Besler, German-Polish composer, born in Brzeg, Duchy of Brezeg (d. 1625)
David Teniers the Younger, Flemish court painter (Theatrum Pictorium), born in Antwerp (d. 1690)
Gregory King, English statistician (Natural & Political Observations), born in Lichfield, England (d. 1712)
Michel-Richard Delalande, French organist and baroque composer (De Profundis), born in Paris, France (d. 1726)
Ludwig Ernst, German composer, born in Gotha, Duchy of Saxe-Gotha (d. 1739)
Philippe-Jacques Pfeffinger, French composer, born in Strasbourg, France (d. 1821)
Godert van der Capellen, Dutch statesman (Governor-General of Dutch-Indies, 1819-26), born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1848)
Edward Coles, American anti-slavery advocate and politician (Governor of Illinois, 1822-26), born in Albemarle County, Virginia (d. 1868)
Charles Cowden Clarke, English editor and Shakespearean critic, born in Enfield, Middlesex, England (d. 1877)
Henry Charles Carey, American economist (Principles of Poli Economy), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1879)
August Freyer, Polish organist (Warsaw Evangelical Church, 1837-83), composer, and pedagogue, born in Mildenau, Electorate of Saxony (d. 1883)
Janos Bolyai, Hungarian mathematician (non-Euclidean geometry), born in Kolozsvár, Hungary (d. 1860)
Isidor Dannström, Swedish composer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1897)
Auguste Vaucorbeil, French composer, born in Rouen, France (d. 1884)
Edward Stephen, Welsh musician and composer, born in Maentwrog, Merionethshire, Wales (d. 1885)
Friedrich Gottlieb Schwencke, German organist and composer, born in Hamburg, German Confederation (d. 1896)
David Butler, American politician (1st Governor of Nebraska, 1867-71), impeached for misuse of funds, born near Linton, Indiana (d. 1891)
Francesco D'Arcais, Italian composer, born in Cagliari, Kingdom of Sardinia (d. 1890)
Edmond Picard, French-Belgian lawyer and writer (La forge Roussel), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1924)
Henry Gadsby, English musician and composer, born in Hackney, England (d. 1907)
Muḥammad Tawfīq Pasha, Khedive of Egypt (1879-92), born in Cairo, Egypt (d. 1892)
Jean Baptiste August Kessler, Dutch entrepreneur and director of oil on Dutch Indies, born in Batavia, Dutch East Indies (d. 1900)
Eugeniusz Pankiewicz, Polish composer, born in Siedlce, Poland (d. 1898)
L. L. Zamenhof, Polish physician and linguist (Esperanto), born in Białystok, Poland, Russian Empire (d. 1917)
Abner Powell, American baseball utility, team owner and executive (first to use infield tarpaulin after rain), born in Shenandoah, Pennsylvania (d. 1953)
Niels Ryberg Finsen, Danish-Farosese physician and phototherapist (Nobel Prize for Medicine 1903 for treatment for Lupus), born in Tórshavn, Faroe Islands (d. 1904)
Charles Duryea, American inventor (Duryea Motor Wagon Company, first auto built in the US), born in Canton, Illinois (d. 1938)
Jack 'The Nonpareil' Dempsey, Irish boxer (first holder of World Middleweight Championship 1886-90), born in Curran, County Kildare (d. 1895)
Arthur Dehon Little, American chemist (patented rayon), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1935)
Josef Hoffmann, Austrian architect (Vienna Secession Movement), born in Brtnice, Moravia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1956)
Kacsóh Pongrác, Hungarian composer, born in Budapest, Austria-Hungary (d. 1923)
Emilio Jacinto, Filipino poet and revolutionary, born in Tondo, Manila (d. 1899)
Friedrich Niggli, Swiss composer, born in Aarburg, Switzerland (d. 1959)
Ferdinand Hardekopf, German writer and journalist, born in Varel, Germany (d. 1954)
Hans Carossa, German writer, born in Tölz, Bavaria (d. 1956)
Hugo Willem Constantijn Bordewijk, Dutch lawyer, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1939)
Rudolf von Laban, Czech-German choreographer (modern dance), born in Bratislava, Austria-Hungary (d. 1958)
James Schneider, American silent film director (A Foolish Romance) and actor (Keystone Kops shorts), born in New York City (d. 1967)
Pieter Geyl, Dutch historian (History of the Dutch Tribe), born in Dordrecht, Netherlands (d. 1966)
Artturi Leinonen, Finnish journalist and writer (Kati), born in Ylihärmä, Finland (d. 1963)
A. P. Carter, American country-folk and gospel singer-songwriter, and guitarist (The Carter Family), born in Maces Spring, Virginia (d. 1960)
David Guion, American composer (Home on the Range), born in Ballinger, Texas (d. 1981)
José María Castro, Argentine composer, born in Avellaneda, Buenos Aires (d. 1964)
Betty Smith, American novelist (A Tree Grows in Brooklyn), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1972)
George Cressey, American geographer (Asia's Lands & Peoples), born in Tiffin, Ohio (d. 1963)
Margaret Bannerman, Canadian actress (Elopement), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 1976)
Paul Citroen, Dutch sculptor, born in Berlin (d. 1983)
Fernando Remacha, Spanish composer, born in Tudela, Navarre, Spain (d. 1984)
Frank Vosper, British actor (The Man Who Knew Too Much, Rome Express), born in London, England (d. 1937)
Harold Abrahams, British athlete (Olympic gold 100m, silver 4x100m 1924; depicted in film "Chariots of Fire"), born in Bedford, England (d. 1978)
Francesco Messina, Italian sculptor, born in Linguaglossa, Italy (d. 1995)
Nudie Cohn, Ukrainian-American tailor (created the rhinestone 'nudie suits' worn by musicians), born in Kiev, Russian Empire (d. 1984)[1]
Robert F. Bradford, American politician (57th Governor of Massachusetts), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1983)
Tamanishiki San'emon, Japanese sumo wrestler (32nd yokozuna; winner 9 top division yūshō), born in Kōchi, Japan (d. 1938)
Kermit Bloomgarden, American producer (The Diary of Anne Frank; The Music Man), born in New York City (d. 1976)
William Hitzig, Austrian Maxwell physician, born in Austria (d. 1983)
Ferenc Farkas, Hungarian composer, born in Nagykanizsa, Hungary (d. 2000)
Irawati Karve, India's first female anthropologist, born in Burma (d. 1970)[1]
Gordon Douglas [Brickner], American film director (Our Gang; Laurel and Hardy; Young At Heart; Way...Way Out; Tony Rome; They Call Me Mister Tibbs!), born in New York City (d. 1993)

John H. Hammond, American music producer, talent scout, promoter, and activist, "discovered" Billie Holiday, Teddy Wilson, Count Basie, Bob Dylan, and Bruce Springsteen, born in New York City (d. 1987)
Nicholas P. Dallis, American psychiatrist and comic strip writer (Rex Morgan, M.D.), born in New York City (d. 1991)
Stan Kenton, American musician, jazz pianist, arranger and bandleader (Artistry In Rhythm), born in Wichita, Kansas (d. 1979)
Ray Eames, American designer and filmmaker, born in Sacramento, California (d. 1988)
Muriel Rukeyser, American poet (The Book of the Dead), born in New York City (d. 1980)
Rik Jacobs, Flemish stage manager, born in Antwerpen, Flanders, Belgium (d. 1985)
Isabel Crook (née Brown), Canadian-British anthropologist, author (Prosperity's Predicament), and professor (Beijing Foreign Studies University. 1947-81), born in Chengdu, Sichuan, China (d. 2023)[1]
José Toribio Merino, Chilean admiral (one of the principal leaders of the 1973 Chilean coup d'état), born in La Serena, Chile (d. 1996)
(Edwin) "Buddy" Cole, American jazz and session pianist, Hammond organist, orchestra leader, and composer ("Truth Or Consequences"), born in Irving, Illinois (d. 1964)
Shan-ul-Haq Haqqee, Pakistani Urdu linguist and writer, born in Delhi, British India (d. 2005)
Jeff Chandler [Ira Grossel], American actor (Broken Arrow), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1961)

Eddie Robinson, American baseball first baseman (MLB All Star 1949, 51–53; Washington Sens, Chicago White Sox, Philadelphia A's) and executive (GM Texas Rangers, Atlanta Braves), born in Paris, Texas (d. 2021)
Howard Cable, Canadian conductor, arranger, and composer, born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 2016)
Kurt Schaffenberger, American comics artist (Superman's Girl Friend, Lois Lane), born in Thuringian Forest, Germany (d. 2002)
(Albert) "Alan" Freed, American disc jockey and concert promoter who popularized the term "rock-n-roll", and payola scandal figure, born in Windber, Pennsylvania (d. 1965)
Freeman Dyson, American physicist (known for his work in quantum electrodynamics, solid-state physics, astronomy and nuclear engineering), born in Crowthorne, Berkshire, England (d. 2020)
Uziel Gal, Israeli firearm designer (Uzi submachine gun), born in Weimar, Germany (d. 2002)
Valentin Varennikov, Soviet-Russian general and statesman, born in Krasnodar, Russian SFSR, Soviet Union (d. 2009)
Esther Bejarano, German accordionist, singer (Coincidence; Microphone Mafia), anti-fascism activist, and Holocaust survivor, born in Saarlouis, Germany (d. 2021)
(Josie) "Kasey" Rogers, American actress (Strangers On A Train; Bewitched - "Louise Tate"), born in Morehouse, Missouri (d. 2006)

Jack Rothstein, British concert violinist (London Czech Trio), conductor (Viennese Orchestra of London), and session player (The Beatles - "I Am the Walrus"; "Within You Without You"; Paul McCartney - “Here Today”), born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 2001)
Sam Pollock, Canadian NHL hockey general manager, 9X Stanley Cups (Montreal Canadiens, 1964-78), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2007)
Gene Quill, American jazz alto saxophonist (Phil Woods, Quincy Jones, Gerry Mulligan), born in Atlantic City, New Jersey (d.1988)
Friedensreich Hundertwasser, Austrian-New Zealand artist and architect (Hundertwasserhaus) known for his work in environmental protection, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 2000)
Ida Haendel, Polish-British-Canadian child prodigy violinist, teacher, and author (Woman with Violin), born in Chelm, Poland (d. 2020)
Jerry Wallace, American country and pop singer ("Primrose Lane"; "If You Leave Me Tonight I'll Cry"), born in Guilford, Missouri (d. 2008)
Jimmy Nelson, American ventriloquist (Nestles - Farfel the Dog), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2019)
(Golden) "Big" Wheeler, American Chicago blues and electric blues singer, harmonic player and songwriter, born in Baconton, Georgia (d.. 1998)
Barry Harris, American bebop jazz pianist (Cannonball Adderly; Lee Morgan; "The Bird of Red and Gold"), and educator, born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 2021)[1]
Keith Andrew, English cricket wicketkeeper (2 Tests; Northamptonshire CCC), born in Oldham, England (d. 2010)
Alimuddin, Pakistani cricket batsman (25 Tests, 2 x 100s, 7 x 50s; Rajputana, Karachi), born in Ajmer, India (d. 2012)
Edna O'Brien, Irish novelist and short story writer (The Country Grils; Girl with Green Eyes; Casualties of Peace), born in Tuamgraney, County Clare, Ireland (d. 2024)[1]
Evald Schorm, Czech film director (Courage for Every Day), born in Prague, Czechoslovakia (d. 1988)
Curtis Fuller, American jazz trombonist (Yusef Lateef; John Coltrane; Farmer/Golson Jazztet), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 2021)
Elaine Barkin (née Radoff), American composer, educator, music writer and editor (Perspectives of New Music, 1964-85), born in The Bronx, New York City (d. 2023)
Igor Štuhec, Slovenian contemporary classical composer (Entuziazmi; Differentiations), born in Kremberk, Yugoslavia (d. 2024)
